does: This paper synthesizes research on how affective cues and emotion-like behaviors in AI agents influence human-AI collaboration dynamics. The authors develop an integrated framework treating affect as a coordination mechanism rather than an internal agent property, examining how model-generated affective signals shape trust calibration, delegation decisions, error correction, and human oversight. The work draws together previously fragmented literatures on affective computing, simulated empathy in language models, trust in automation, and AI safety to explain the concrete interactional mechanisms through which emotional displays influence reliance, repair behaviors, and responsibility negotiation in agentic systems where humans delegate and monitor consequential tasks.
connects: This review builds on longstanding work in human-computer interaction and trust research examining how users calibrate reliance on automated systems, extending it to modern autonomous agents with memory, tool-use and partial autonomy. It engages affective computing traditions studying emotion recognition and generation, while connecting to recent work on whether large language models exhibit or simulate empathy and how their outputs affect human perception. The paper situates itself within AI safety discourse on human oversight and error correction, and within broader literature on governance of increasingly autonomous systems, synthesizing insights from automation psychology about trust, anthropomorphism, and appropriate dependence.
enables: This framework enables better design of collaborative AI systems by clarifying which affective signals support appropriate trust calibration versus problematic over-reliance or learned helplessness. It points toward interventions that use affect strategically for coordination—signaling uncertainty, requesting human judgment, or scaffolding repair—without manipulation. The work supports development of governance mechanisms that leverage affective interaction loops for meaningful human oversight rather than performative compliance. Future research could empirically test how different emotional displays affect delegation and correction behaviors, how affective signals interact with agent transparency and explainability, and how to maintain human agency in increasingly agentic systems through affect-based coordination.
